content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 7, 1990


Manuel G. Nunez
4983 E. Grant
Fresno, CA 93727

Re: Your Request For Advice
Our File No. A-90-522

Dear Mr. Nunez:

This is in response to your letter of August 4, 1990,
requesting confirmation of telephone advice concerning the
campaign financing provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹

QUESTION

You have inquired whether you, as an elected Trustee of the
Fresno Unified School Board, may use your campaign funds to pay
for the cost of defending a case in which your election is
contested.

CONCLUSION

The Act does not prohibit the use of an elected official's
campaign funds for the cost of defending a case in which that
official's election is contested. However, because other legal
provisions could impact on that situation, you should contact your
board or county counsel as to those matters.

FACTS

On June 5, 1990, you were elected to the position of Fresno
Unified School District Trustee. An unsuccessful candidate in
your area is now contesting the election.


¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-90-522
Page 2

ANALYSIS

Section 85202 provides that campaign funds are held in trust
for expenses associated with the election of the candidate.
Article 8 (Section 85800 - 85802) specifies the limitations on the
use of those funds. Generally, funds may be used by an elected
officer for purposes reasonably related to a legislative or
governmental purpose. (Section 85801)

Section 85802.5 provides that:

"(a) Expenditures of campaign funds for attorney's
fees and costs in connection with ... litigation are not
directly related to a political, legislative or
governmental purpose except where the litigation arises
directly out of a(n) .... elected officer's ... status as
a(n) ... elected officer, including, ... an action
arising from an election contest or recount."

[Emphasis added.]

Thus, the use of campaign funds for attorney fees and other
litigation expenses in the defense of your status as Trustee is
not prohibited by the Political Reform Act.

Because the advice provided by the Fair Political Practices
Commission is limited to the provisions of the Act, other legal
provisions, such as any local ordinances which may have some
bearing on the situation, are beyond the scope of this letter.
You should contact the school board's attorney for advice as to
these matters.

I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this mat-
ter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Joseph Garcia
Senior Commission Counsel,
Legal Division
